title: Ingress Controllers and Annotations
description:
Explore the Ingress Controller ecosystem, how annotations extend controller behavior, and how multiple Ingress resources are merged into one running proxy configuration.

## Overview
While the Ingress API is standardized, the behavior of advanced features is delegated to the controller implementation. Annotations are the primary escape hatch for controller-specific configuration that the Ingress spec itself does not cover. Understanding annotations — and their scope — prevents hours of debugging.

## Common Ingress Controllers

| Controller | Backing Proxy | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **ingress-nginx** | Nginx | Most popular; enormous annotation library |
| **Traefik** | Traefik | Native Kubernetes CRDs; great for dynamic config |
| **Contour** | Envoy | HTTPProxy CRD; strong gRPC/HTTP2 |
| **HAProxy Ingress** | HAProxy | High throughput, fine-grained TCP tuning |

Install exactly one controller per `IngressClass` name. Multiple controllers can coexist in the same cluster using different class names.

## Annotations — Controller-Specific Config
Annotations live in `metadata.annotations` and are read exclusively by the controller that owns the Ingress. nginx-ingress examples:

```yaml
metadata:
  annotations:
    # Strip path prefix before forwarding to backend
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /$2
    # Limit request body size (default 1m)
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size: "10m"
    # Force HTTP → HTTPS redirect
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"
    # Rate limiting
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/limit-rps: "20"
    # Basic auth
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/auth-type: basic
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/auth-secret: basic-auth-secret
```

## Path Rewriting with Capture Groups
A common source of confusion: you want requests to `/app/foo` forwarded as `/foo` to the backend. Use a capture group in `path` and reference it in `rewrite-target`:

```yaml
metadata:
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /$2
spec:
  rules:
  - host: example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /app(/|$)(.*)
        pathType: ImplementationSpecific
        backend:
          service:
            name: my-service
            port:
              number: 80
```

Here `$2` captures everything after `/app`, so `/app/users` becomes `/users` at the backend. Without this, `/app/users` reaches the backend as `/app/users`, which most apps do not expect.

## Multiple Ingress Resources
Controllers merge all Ingress resources in all namespaces into a single nginx/Envoy configuration. Rules are combined — each Ingress contributes its host+path entries. This means:
- Two teams can own separate Ingress resources for the same controller
- Conflicting rules (same host + same path in two Ingress resources) cause unpredictable behavior — the controller may pick one silently
- Use namespaces and RBAC to prevent teams from accidentally conflicting

## IngressClass Resource
`IngressClass` decouples the controller from the Ingress resource and allows multiple controllers to coexist:

```yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IngressClass
metadata:
  name: nginx-internal
  annotations:
    ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: "false"
spec:
  controller: k8s.io/ingress-nginx
  parameters:
    apiGroup: k8s.example.com
    kind: IngressParameters
    name: nginx-internal-params
```

Set `ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: "true"` on one class to make it the default for Ingresses that omit `spec.ingressClassName`.

## Default Backend
Any request that does not match a defined host+path rule falls through to the `defaultBackend`. Configure it at the controller level (applies globally) or per Ingress resource. A simple catch-all returning HTTP 404 or a branded error page is best practice.

## Common Pitfalls
- **Annotations are controller-scoped**: nginx annotations on a Traefik-controlled Ingress are silently ignored. Always confirm which controller manages the resource.
- **rewrite-target capture group gotcha**: if you add a capture group to `path` but forget to update `rewrite-target`, Nginx returns a 500 because `$2` is undefined.
- **Conflicting rules**: same host+path in two Ingress objects → one silently wins. Use `kubectl describe ingress` to see which backend is active.
- **No default IngressClass**: if `spec.ingressClassName` is omitted and no default class exists, the Ingress is orphaned.

quizzes.question: An Ingress has the annotation nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true" but the cluster uses Traefik as its controller. What happens?

options.text: Traefik reads the annotation and enables SSL redirect
The Ingress fails to apply and returns a validation error
The annotation is silently ignored by Traefik
Kubernetes automatically translates the annotation to Traefik format

quizzes.question: What is the purpose of the IngressClass resource?

options.text: It defines which Pods are allowed to receive Ingress traffic
It maps a class name to a specific Ingress Controller, allowing multiple controllers to coexist in one cluster
It replaces the need for annotations on Ingress resources
It is only needed when using TLS termination

quizzes.question: You route /app(.*) to a backend but requests arrive with the /app prefix intact. Which annotation fixes this for nginx-ingress?

options.text: nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/backend-protocol

quizzes.question: Two Ingress resources in different namespaces define the same host (example.com) and path (/api) for the same nginx-ingress controller. What is the most likely result?

options.text: Both Ingress resources are invalid and rejected
Traffic is load-balanced across both backends equally
One backend silently wins; behavior is unpredictable
Kubernetes creates a merged Ingress resource automatically

quizzes.question: Which annotation marks an IngressClass as the default for Ingresses that omit spec.ingressClassName?

options.text: ingressclass.kubernetes.io/default: "true"
ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: "true"
spec.defaultClass: true in the IngressClass
kubernetes.io/ingress-class: default

quizzes.question: In nginx-ingress path rewriting, what does the capture group $2 represent in rewrite-target: /$2 when path is /app(/|$)(.*)?

options.text: The entire original path including /app
The first capture group, which is / or empty string
Everything after /app, effectively stripping the /app prefix
The hostname of the request

quiz_explanations.q1: Annotations are controller-specific and are only meaningful to the controller that implements them. Traefik does not read nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/* annotations. They are stored in the Ingress metadata but completely ignored by Traefik, with no error or warning.
quiz_explanations.q2: IngressClass resources register a named class associated with a specific controller implementation. Multiple controllers (e.g., one for internal traffic, one for external) can run in the same cluster, each owning Ingresses that reference their respective class names.
quiz_explanations.q3: nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target allows you to rewrite the upstream URL path before forwarding to the backend. Combined with a regex capture group in spec.rules[].http.paths[].path, you can strip prefixes from the URL.
quiz_explanations.q4: nginx-ingress merges all Ingress resources into one Nginx configuration. When two rules conflict (same host + path), the controller picks one — typically the oldest or alphabetically first — silently. The other rule is dropped, causing hard-to-debug routing failures.
quiz_explanations.q5: Setting the annotation ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: 'true' on an IngressClass resource makes it the cluster default. Any Ingress created without spec.ingressClassName will be assigned to this class automatically.
quiz_explanations.q6: In the regex /app(/|$)(.*), $1 is the first group (/ or end of string) and $2 is the second group capturing everything after /app. So rewrite-target: /$2 effectively strips the /app prefix: /app/users becomes /users at the backend.
